records.ety: [OE. ploungen, OF. plongier, F. plonger, fr. (assumed) LL. plumbicare, fr. L. plumbum lead. See Plumb.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Plunged (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Plunging (?).]

defs: To thrust into water, or into any substance that is penetrable; to immerse; to cause to penetrate or enter quickly and forcibly; to thrust; as, to plunge the body into water; to plunge a dagger into the breast. Also used figuratively; as, to plunge a nation into war.
quotes: Bound and plunged him into a cell.
We shall be plunged into perpetual errors.

defs: To entangle; to embarrass; to overcome.
quotes: Plunged and graveled with three lines of Seneca.

defs: To thrust or cast one's self into water or other fluid; to submerge one's self; to dive, or to rush in; as, he plunged into the river. Also used figuratively; as, to plunge into debt.
quotes: Forced to plunge naked in the raging sea.
To plunge into guilt of a murther.

defs: To pitch or throw one's self headlong or violently forward, as a horse does.
quotes: Some wild colt, which . . . flings and plunges.

defs: To bet heavily and with seeming recklessness on a race, or other contest; in an extended sense, to risk large sums in hazardous speculations.
The act of thrusting into or submerging; a dive, leap, rush, or pitch into, or as into, water; as, to take the water with a plunge.
Hence, a desperate hazard or act; a state of being submerged or overwhelmed with difficulties.
quotes: She was brought to that plunge, to conceal her husband's murder or accuse her son.
And with thou not reach out a friendly arm, To raise me from amidst this plunge of sorrows?

defs: The act of pitching or throwing one's self headlong or violently forward, like an unruly horse.
Heavy and reckless betting in horse racing; hazardous speculation.
